narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.20—right around the typical 3.21 average for students in this dataset—your law school application was evaluated using a predictive model that assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try. The model estimated a 51% probability that you would fail the bar exam, which crossed the threshold for rejection, as the school prioritizes admitting students predicted to pass with high confidence. The primary driver pushing the prediction toward failure was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, which is notably lower than the average of 5.53; this early indicator of academic performance in later undergraduate years had the strongest influence, signaling potential challenges in sustaining high achievement through law school and the bar. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA exerted a substantial counterbalancing force toward predicting success, helping to mitigate some of the risk. Your middle family income bracket also nudged the prediction slightly in favor of passing, as it aligns with many students who succeed despite not coming from the highest economic backgrounds. Compared to typical full-time students—who make up over 92% of the dataset—your planned full-time status didn't stand out as unusual, but it wasn't enough to offset the top concerns. Your LSAT score of 14.50, well below the average of 36.70, played a supporting role in the overall risk profile but wasn't among the most decisive factors here. While your profile shows strengths like that near-average GPA, the model weighed your lower Year 3 decile most heavily, as it often correlates with bar exam outcomes for applicants in similar situations. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a white male student from a middle-income background—many with comparable profiles improve with targeted effort. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to the average, strengthening your Year 3 performance through additional coursework or tutoring, and gathering strong letters of recommendation that highlight your resilience. Reapplying after addressing these areas could shift your predicted probability toward passing, opening doors at this or other programs. You're clearly motivated, and with focused steps, you can rewrite this prediction in your favor.
